verbatim_text: Welcome to the colloquium, everyone. Our speaker today is Belinda Li. She is a PhD student at MIT EECS where she works with Jacob Andreas. She's won a number of awards, for example, the Rising Star Award in EECS, the Claire Booth Luce Award. And today in her talk, she talked about her research on building AI systems that model users, the world and themselves. Okay, so let's welcome Belinda. Yeah, thank you so much for having me. I'm super excited to be here. So in this talk, I'm going to be motivating for why I think it's important to build AI system with coherent and updatable models of the world, the user and themselves. And I'll also talk about how we can actually do this. So, okay, these models, the world, the user and self models, they allow AI systems to act in accordance with the external environment to adapt to different users and to remain self consistent and transparent. And ultimately, I think this leads us to a future of more reliable, adaptive, transparent, and collaborative AI systems. But to truly contextualize what these models are, let's take a look at where this field has been. So each era of AI had a very different understanding of a model.
